Parallax error occurs when a scale is viewed from an angle instead of along a line perpendicular to the scale at the point of measurement. Because the eye is not directly above the mark being read, the apparent position of the object shifts relative to the graduations, leading to an incorrect reading.

A beginner may ask: 'Why do different observers obtain different readings?' Each observer may place their eye at a different angle, causing the mark to appear against different scale divisions.

Another beginner may ask: 'How can this be prevented?' The observer should position the eye directly above the point being read so that the line of sight is perpendicular to the scale. This removes the apparent shift and gives the true reading.

Parallax error is common when using metre rules, thermometers, measuring cylinders, analogue ammeters and voltmeters. JAMB frequently tests this concept through practical laboratory situations rather than by asking for its definition.
steps:
Step 1: Identify the problem described in the experiment.

Different observers obtain different readings because they view the scale from different positions.

Step 2: Recognise the type of measurement error.

This is parallax error, caused by viewing the scale from an angle.

Step 3: Recall the correct laboratory technique.

The eye should be placed directly above the graduation being read so that the line of sight is perpendicular to the scale.

Step 4: Evaluate the options.

Only one option correctly describes this standard laboratory practice.
Step 5: Therefore, the correct procedure is to view the graduation directly above the end of the brass strip with the eye perpendicular to the metre rule.
why_other_options_are_wrong: A. Hold the metre rule at an angle to the brass strip while taking the reading. — Tilting the metre rule can introduce additional measurement errors instead of eliminating parallax.
B. View the graduation directly above the end of the brass strip with the eye perpendicular to the scale. — Correct. This is the standard method of eliminating parallax error.
C. Take the reading from either end of the metre rule and calculate the average. — Averaging does not remove parallax if every reading is taken incorrectly.
D. Increase the distance between the eye and the metre rule before reading the scale. — Moving farther away does not eliminate parallax; correct eye alignment is what matters.
explanation.exam_tip: Whenever a question mentions that different observers obtain different readings because they look from different positions, think of parallax error. The correct remedy is always to position the eye directly above the mark being read (line of sight perpendicular to the scale). Do not confuse this with calibration errors or zero error, which have different causes.
explanation.final_answer: B. View the graduation directly above the end of the brass strip with the eye perpendicular to the scale.
explanation.simple_explanation: The observers obtained different readings because they looked at the metre rule from different angles. To avoid parallax error, the eye must be placed directly above the graduation being read. This ensures the object lines up with the correct scale mark.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Errors, Accuracy and Significant Figures → Parallax Error to learn how incorrect eye position affects measurements on metre rules, thermometers, measuring cylinders, analogue ammeters and voltmeters. Practise more JAMB-style laboratory questions on avoiding observational errors and improving measurement accuracy.

question:
A physics laboratory group measured the time taken for a simple pendulum to complete 20 oscillations five times under the same conditions. Their results were recorded as shown below:

Trial 1: 35.8 s
Trial 2: 35.9 s
Trial 3: 35.8 s
Trial 4: 39.6 s
Trial 5: 35.9 s

Before calculating the average period of the pendulum, the laboratory supervisor advised the students to improve the reliability of their result. Which of the following actions should they take first?

options.A: Include all five readings and calculate the mean immediately.
options.B: Discard the obviously inconsistent reading and calculate the mean using the remaining readings.
options.C: Replace the largest reading with the smallest reading before calculating the mean.
options.D: Use only the first reading because it was recorded before fatigue could affect the experiment.

answer_text: Discard the obviously inconsistent reading and calculate the mean using the remaining readings.

explanation.question_restate: Five measurements of the time for 20 oscillations were taken. Four readings are closely grouped around 35.8–35.9 s, while one reading (39.6 s) is much larger than the others. The task is to identify the best procedure for obtaining a more reliable result.
explanation.principle:
Reliable experimental results are obtained when repeated measurements are consistent with one another. An observation that differs markedly from the rest is called an outlier. Outliers may arise from mistakes such as incorrect timing, distraction, or improper observation. Before calculating the mean, experimental data should be examined to determine whether any value is clearly inconsistent with the others.

A beginner may ask, 'Why not always use every reading?' The answer is that an obvious outlier can distort the average and make the reported result less representative of the repeated measurements.

Another beginner may ask, 'Can we remove any reading we do not like?' No. A reading should only be rejected when there is good evidence that it is inconsistent with the rest of the data or resulted from an identifiable experimental error.
steps:
Step 1: Compare the five readings.

35.8 s, 35.9 s, 35.8 s and 35.9 s agree closely with one another.

Step 2: Identify the inconsistent value.

39.6 s is much larger than the other four readings and is an obvious outlier.

Step 3: Decide how to improve the reliability of the result.

The outlier should be excluded before calculating the mean because it is not consistent with the repeated measurements.

Step 4: Calculate the mean using only the consistent readings.

Mean time = (35.8 + 35.9 + 35.8 + 35.9) ÷ 4 = 35.85 s.
Step 5: Therefore, the correct first action is to discard the inconsistent reading and use the remaining consistent readings to obtain a more reliable result.
why_other_options_are_wrong: A. Include all five readings and calculate the mean immediately. — Incorrect. The outlier would significantly distort the average and reduce the reliability of the reported result.

B. Discard the obviously inconsistent reading and calculate the mean using the remaining readings. — Correct. Removing a clear outlier before averaging produces a result that better represents the repeated observations.

C. Replace the largest reading with the smallest reading before calculating the mean. — Incorrect. Experimental data must never be altered or invented. Measurements should only be accepted or rejected based on valid experimental reasons.

D. Use only the first reading because it was recorded before fatigue could affect the experiment. — Incorrect. Reliability is improved by considering repeated consistent measurements, not by relying on a single observation.
explanation.exam_tip: In JAMB practical questions, look for measurements that are tightly clustered together. If one reading is clearly different from the rest, it is usually an outlier. Reliable results are obtained by repeating measurements, identifying obvious outliers where justified, and averaging the consistent values rather than relying on a single reading.
explanation.final_answer: B. Discard the obviously inconsistent reading and calculate the mean using the remaining readings.
explanation.simple_explanation: Four of the measurements are almost identical, while one value (39.6 s) is far from the others. This makes it an outlier. To obtain a more reliable result, the outlier should be rejected before calculating the average of the consistent readings.
explanation.tutorial_reference:
Visit Tutorials → Physics → Errors, Accuracy and Significant Figures → Reliability and Repeatability to learn how scientists analyse repeated measurements, identify outliers, improve experimental reliability, interpret laboratory data and answer JAMB-style practical questions involving repeated observations and mean values.

Need more help? Explore the Tutorial Section inside NaijaPrizeGate CBT and Learning Centre for comprehensive lessons, worked examples, examiner tips and thousands of carefully structured JAMB preparation questions.

question:
During a practical exercise, four groups independently measured the time taken for a metal pendulum to complete 20 oscillations under identical laboratory conditions. Their results are shown below:

Group A: 39.8 s, 40.0 s, 39.9 s, 40.1 s
Group B: 37.2 s, 41.8 s, 39.4 s, 40.6 s
Group C: 40.0 s, 40.0 s, 40.0 s, 40.0 s
Group D: 38.9 s, 39.1 s, 41.0 s, 39.0 s

Ignoring the true value of the period, which group produced the most reliable set of measurements?

explanation.question_restate: Four laboratory groups repeated the same pendulum experiment several times. Since the accepted (true) value is not provided, the task is to identify the group whose repeated measurements are the most reliable by examining the consistency of the results.
explanation.principle:
Reliability in experimental measurements refers to the ability of an experiment or measuring procedure to produce consistent results when repeated under the same conditions. A highly reliable experiment gives readings that agree closely with one another.

A beginner may ask: 'Do I need the correct value to judge reliability?' No. Reliability is based on consistency (repeatability), not closeness to the true value. Accuracy requires knowledge of the accepted value, but reliability can be assessed simply by examining how closely repeated measurements agree.

One useful way to judge reliability is by considering the spread (range) of the readings. The smaller the spread, the more repeatable and therefore more reliable the measurements are.
steps: Step 1: Examine the readings for each group and compare how closely they agree.

Step 2: Determine the spread (range) of each set.

Group A: 40.1 − 39.8 = 0.3 s.

Step 3: Group B: 41.8 − 37.2 = 4.6 s.

This large variation indicates poor repeatability.

Step 4: Group C: 40.0 − 40.0 = 0.0 s.

All four readings are identical, showing perfect consistency.

Step 5: Group D: 41.0 − 38.9 = 2.1 s.

The readings vary considerably more than those of Groups A and C.

Step 6: Compare the ranges.

Group C has the smallest possible spread (0.0 s), indicating the highest repeatability and reliability.
Step 7: Therefore, Group C produced the most reliable set of measurements.
why_other_options_are_wrong: A. Group A — The readings are fairly consistent, but they still vary by 0.3 s. They are less repeatable than Group C.
B. Group B — Incorrect. The measurements show a very large spread of 4.6 s, indicating poor reliability.
C. Group C — Correct. Every reading is identical, giving the greatest consistency and therefore the highest reliability.
D. Group D — Incorrect. The spread of 2.1 s is much larger than that of Groups A and C, showing lower repeatability.
explanation.exam_tip: In JAMB practical questions, reliability is usually assessed from repeated measurements rather than by comparing with the true value. When several readings are given, first check how closely they agree. A smaller spread (range) generally indicates better repeatability and higher reliability. Do not confuse reliability with accuracy; an experiment can produce highly consistent results yet still be inaccurate if there is a systematic error.
explanation.simple_explanation: Reliable measurements are those that can be repeated with almost the same result. Group C obtained exactly 40.0 s in all four trials, meaning there was no variation between the readings. This makes Group C's measurements the most reliable.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Errors, Accuracy and Significant Figures → Reliability and Repeatability to learn how repeated measurements are analysed, how to identify outliers, how consistency differs from accuracy, and how scientists improve the reliability of laboratory experiments. Practise additional JAMB-style questions involving repeated observations, pendulum experiments, stopwatch measurements and laboratory data interpretation.

question:
During a practical exercise, four groups independently measured the time taken for a simple pendulum to complete 20 oscillations under the same laboratory conditions. Their results are shown below.

Group A: 36.2 s, 36.3 s, 36.2 s
Group B: 35.1 s, 36.7 s, 37.5 s
Group C: 36.2 s, 36.2 s, 36.2 s
Group D: 34.8 s, 36.2 s, 37.6 s

Without knowing the true value of the time, which group produced the most reliable set of measurements?

explanation.question_restate: Four groups repeated the same pendulum timing experiment three times. The task is to identify the group whose measurements are the most reliable based solely on the consistency of the repeated readings.
explanation.principle:
Reliability refers to the consistency of repeated measurements obtained under the same conditions. When an experiment is repeated several times, reliable measurements show very little variation from one reading to another.

A beginner may ask: 'Why is the true value not needed?' Reliability is different from accuracy. Accuracy compares a measurement with the accepted or true value, whereas reliability examines how closely repeated measurements agree with one another.

Another beginner may ask: 'What indicates high reliability?' A small spread (or range) between repeated readings indicates that random errors are minimal and the experiment is repeatable.

The range is calculated as:

Range = Highest reading − Lowest reading.

The group with the smallest range has the highest reliability.
steps: Step 1: Determine the spread of readings for each group.
Step 2: Group A: Range = 36.3 − 36.2 = 0.1 s.
Step 3: Group B: Range = 37.5 − 35.1 = 2.4 s.
Step 4: Group C: Range = 36.2 − 36.2 = 0.0 s because all three readings are identical.
Step 5: Group D: Range = 37.6 − 34.8 = 2.8 s.
Step 6: Compare the ranges. Group C has the smallest possible range (0.0 s), showing perfect repeatability.
Step 7: Therefore, Group C produced the most reliable measurements.
why_other_options_are_wrong: A. Group A — The readings are fairly consistent, but there is still a small variation of 0.1 s. They are less reliable than Group C.
B. Group B — The readings are widely scattered, indicating poor repeatability and significant random variation.
C. Group C — Correct. All three measurements are identical, giving a range of 0.0 s and demonstrating the highest reliability.
D. Group D — The measurements have the largest spread, showing the least consistent results among the four groups.
explanation.exam_tip: In JAMB practical questions, reliability is judged by the consistency of repeated measurements, not by closeness to the true value. Whenever several readings are provided, first examine how closely they agree. The set with the smallest spread or range is the most reliable, even if the true value is unknown.
explanation.simple_explanation: Reliable measurements are those that can be repeated with nearly the same result each time. Group C obtained exactly the same value (36.2 s) in all three trials, so its measurements are the most consistent. Hence, Group C has the highest reliability.
explanation.tutorial_reference:
Visit Tutorials → Physics → Errors, Accuracy and Significant Figures → Reliability and Repeatability to learn how repeated observations improve confidence in experimental results, distinguish reliability from accuracy, interpret laboratory data, and answer JAMB-style practical questions involving repeated measurements and error analysis.

Need more help? Explore the Tutorial Section inside NaijaPrizeGate CBT and Learning Centre for comprehensive lessons, worked examples, examiner tips and thousands of carefully structured JAMB preparation questions.

question: A laboratory assistant measured the time taken for a pendulum to complete 20 oscillations five times under identical conditions. The recorded times were 36.4 s, 36.5 s, 36.3 s, 41.8 s and 36.4 s. Before calculating the average time, the supervisor advised that the reliability of the results should first be considered. Which action would most improve the reliability of the reported result?

options.A: Use only the largest recorded value because it represents the longest observation.
options.B: Calculate the mean using all five readings without examining the unusual value.
options.C: Treat 41.8 s as an outlier, repeat the measurement, and calculate the mean from consistent readings.
options.D: Replace every reading with the median value of 36.4 s without taking additional measurements.

answer_text: Treat 41.8 s as an outlier, repeat the measurement, and calculate the mean from consistent readings.

explanation.question_restate: Five repeated measurements of the time for 20 pendulum oscillations were obtained. Four readings are closely grouped around 36.4 s, while one reading (41.8 s) is far from the others. The task is to identify the best procedure for improving the reliability of the reported result.
explanation.principle:
Reliable measurements are consistent when the same experiment is repeated under identical conditions. When repeated readings cluster closely together, the results are considered repeatable. A value that differs greatly from the rest is called an outlier and may arise from a mistake such as poor timing, distraction, or incorrect observation.

Before calculating a final average, an experimenter should investigate an outlier instead of accepting or discarding it automatically. The best practice is to repeat the measurement. If the repeated value agrees with the consistent readings, the outlier is excluded from the final analysis because it does not represent the normal behaviour of the experiment.

Reliability is improved by obtaining several consistent measurements and using their mean.
steps:
Step 1: Examine the pattern of the readings.

36.4 s, 36.5 s, 36.3 s and 36.4 s are closely grouped.

Step 2: Identify any reading that differs significantly.

41.8 s is much larger than the other four readings, making it a likely outlier.

Step 3: Consider the correct experimental practice.

An outlier should be investigated by repeating the measurement rather than accepting or rejecting it immediately.
Step 4: If the repeated measurement agrees with the clustered values, calculate the mean using the consistent readings.
Step 5: This procedure produces a result that is more reliable because it is based on repeatable observations.
why_other_options_are_wrong: A. Use only the largest recorded value because it represents the longest observation. — Incorrect. A single unusually large reading is not necessarily the most reliable and is likely to be an outlier.
B. Calculate the mean using all five readings without examining the unusual value. — Incorrect. Including a suspected outlier without investigation can produce a misleading average.
C. Treat 41.8 s as an outlier, repeat the measurement, and calculate the mean from consistent readings. — Correct. This is standard laboratory practice for improving reliability and repeatability.

D. Replace every reading with the median value of 36.4 s without taking additional measurements. — Incorrect. A representative value should not be substituted for actual experimental data without further observation.
explanation.exam_tip: In JAMB practical questions, reliability is usually assessed through repeated measurements. When one value is far from the others, first recognise it as a possible outlier, repeat the measurement if possible, and base the final result on consistent observations rather than blindly averaging every value.
explanation.final_answer: C. Treat 41.8 s as an outlier, repeat the measurement, and calculate the mean from consistent readings.
explanation.simple_explanation: Most of the measurements are around 36.4 s, but one reading (41.8 s) is very different. This unusual value is an outlier. To improve the reliability of the experiment, the measurement should be repeated and the final average obtained from readings that agree closely with one another.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Errors, Accuracy and Significant Figures → Reliability and Repeatability to learn how repeated measurements, outlier detection, averaging, and good laboratory practice improve the quality of experimental results. Study additional JAMB-style practical questions involving stopwatches, pendulums, laboratory data analysis, and experimental consistency.

question:
While determining the time taken for a pendulum to complete 20 oscillations, four laboratory groups obtained the following results:

Group I: 40.2 s
Group II: 40.1 s
Group III: 40.3 s
Group IV: 44.8 s

The class teacher suspected that one measurement resulted from a mistake during observation. To improve the reliability of the reported experimental value, which procedure should be adopted?

options.A: Calculate the mean using all four readings.
options.B: Use only the highest reading because it is the latest observation.
options.C: Discard the inconsistent reading and calculate the mean of the remaining readings.
options.D: Report the lowest reading since it is least affected by reaction time.

answer_text: Discard the inconsistent reading and calculate the mean of the remaining readings.

explanation.question_restate: Four groups measured the time for 20 oscillations of a pendulum. Three readings are very close to one another, while one reading is much larger than the others. The task is to identify the best procedure for improving the reliability of the reported result.
explanation.principle:
Reliability refers to the degree to which repeated measurements produce consistent results. When several readings cluster closely together but one differs greatly from the rest, the unusual value is called an outlier. Outliers commonly arise from mistakes such as incorrect stopwatch operation, miscounting oscillations, or recording errors.

A beginner may ask: 'Why not average all the readings?' Including an outlier shifts the mean away from the true value and reduces the reliability of the final result.

A beginner may also ask: 'Can we always discard a reading?' No. A reading should only be rejected when there is good evidence that it is inconsistent with the rest of the data or when a known experimental mistake occurred.

In practical physics, improving reliability involves repeating measurements, identifying inconsistent values where justified, and calculating the mean of the consistent readings.
steps:
Step 1: Examine the pattern of the readings.

40.2 s, 40.1 s and 40.3 s are closely grouped.

Step 2: Identify any reading that differs markedly from the others.

44.8 s is much larger than the other three readings and is an outlier.

Step 3: Decide on the most appropriate treatment of the outlier.

Since it is inconsistent with the rest of the measurements, it should be excluded after confirming that it likely resulted from an observational error.

Step 4: Calculate the mean using only the consistent readings.

This produces a result that better represents repeated measurements and therefore improves reliability.
Step 5: Hence, the correct procedure is to discard the inconsistent reading and average the remaining consistent values.
why_other_options_are_wrong: A. Calculate the mean using all four readings. — Incorrect. Including the obvious outlier gives a less reliable average.
B. Use only the highest reading because it is the latest observation. — Incorrect. The order of observation does not determine reliability, and the highest reading is inconsistent with the others.
C. Discard the inconsistent reading and calculate the mean of the remaining readings. — Correct. This is the accepted approach when a justified outlier is identified.

D. Report the lowest reading since it is least affected by reaction time. — Incorrect. There is no evidence that the lowest reading is the most accurate; a single reading should not replace the mean of consistent repeated measurements.
explanation.exam_tip: In JAMB practical questions, reliability is usually assessed through repeated observations rather than definitions. When most readings agree closely but one differs significantly, first identify the outlier, then determine whether there is a valid reason to exclude it before calculating the mean. Do not assume that the highest or lowest reading is automatically the correct one.
explanation.final_answer: C. Discard the inconsistent reading and calculate the mean of the remaining readings.
explanation.simple_explanation: Three groups obtained almost the same result (40.1 s, 40.2 s and 40.3 s), showing good agreement. The reading of 44.8 s is far from the others and is likely an outlier caused by an experimental mistake. To obtain a more reliable value, the outlier should be discarded and the mean of the consistent readings calculated.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Errors, Accuracy and Significant Figures → Reliability and Repeatability to learn how repeated measurements improve experimental quality, how to recognise outliers, when readings may be rejected, and how reliable laboratory results are reported. Explore additional JAMB-style practical questions involving pendulums, stopwatches, repeated observations and data analysis in the NaijaPrizeGate CBT and Learning Centre.

question: A laboratory beam balance was later discovered to have a calibration fault that caused every mass measurement to be 0.50 g greater than the true value, regardless of the object placed on it. Several groups unknowingly used the balance throughout a practical session. Which statement best describes the error introduced into their measurements?

options.A: It is a random error because different objects have different masses.
options.B: It is a systematic error because every reading is shifted by the same amount in the same direction.
options.C: It is a gross error because students failed to read the balance correctly.
options.D: It is a parallax error because the balance pointer was viewed from different angles.

answer_text: It is a systematic error because every reading is shifted by the same amount in the same direction.

explanation.question_restate: A beam balance has a calibration defect that adds 0.50 g to every mass measurement. The task is to identify the type of error produced by this defect.
explanation.principle:
A systematic error is a consistent error that affects measurements in the same way each time an observation is made. It usually arises from faults such as poor instrument calibration, worn measuring scales, incorrect zero settings or unsuitable experimental procedures.

A beginner may ask: 'Why is it called systematic?' It is called systematic because the error follows a consistent pattern. Every reading is affected in the same direction, either always too high or always too low.

Another beginner may ask: 'How is this different from a random error?' Random errors vary unpredictably from one measurement to another. Sometimes they increase a reading and sometimes they decrease it. In contrast, a systematic error introduces the same bias into every measurement.

In this question, the balance always adds 0.50 g to the true mass. Since every reading is consistently higher by the same amount, the measurements are biased in one direction. This is the defining characteristic of a systematic error caused by faulty calibration.
steps:
Step 1: Identify the source of the error.

The beam balance has a calibration fault.

Step 2: Observe how the fault affects measurements.

Every reading is increased by exactly 0.50 g.

Step 3: Determine whether the error changes from one measurement to another.

It does not. The error remains constant for every object measured.

Step 4: Recall the definition of a systematic error.

A systematic error consistently shifts all measurements in the same direction by a predictable amount.

Step 5: Match the observation with the definition.

Since every reading is consistently too high by 0.50 g, the error is systematic.
Step 6: Therefore, the correct description is that the calibration fault produces a systematic error.
why_other_options_are_wrong: A. It is a random error because different objects have different masses. — Incorrect. The masses of the objects are irrelevant. The balance adds the same 0.50 g to every measurement, so the error is not random.
B. It is a systematic error because every reading is shifted by the same amount in the same direction. — Correct. A constant calibration bias is a classic example of a systematic error.
C. It is a gross error because students failed to read the balance correctly. — Incorrect. The question states that the instrument itself is faulty, not that the users made careless mistakes.

D. It is a parallax error because the balance pointer was viewed from different angles. — Incorrect. Parallax error results from incorrect eye position while taking a reading, not from a calibration defect.
explanation.exam_tip: JAMB commonly tests systematic errors through practical situations involving faulty calibration, incorrect zero settings, stretched metre rules, poorly calibrated thermometers and defective balances. When an instrument causes every reading to be consistently too high or too low, think 'systematic error.' A useful clue is the presence of words such as 'every reading,' 'always,' 'constant,' or 'fixed amount.'
explanation.final_answer: B. It is a systematic error because every reading is shifted by the same amount in the same direction.
explanation.simple_explanation: The beam balance always adds 0.50 g to every measurement. Since the error is the same for all readings and always makes them too high, it is a systematic error caused by poor calibration.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Errors, Accuracy and Significant Figures → Systematic Errors to learn how calibration faults, zero errors, instrument bias and experimental design affect measurement accuracy. Explore additional JAMB-style practical questions involving beam balances, thermometers, metre rules, vernier calipers and other laboratory instruments.

question: A laboratory beam balance was later discovered to have been improperly calibrated and consistently measured every object's mass as 0.25 g greater than its actual value. During an experiment, all mass measurements were taken using this balance. Which statement best describes the effect of this fault on the recorded measurements?

options.A: It introduces a systematic error because every measurement is shifted by the same amount.
options.B: It introduces a random error because the readings vary unpredictably from one measurement to another.
options.C: It introduces a parallax error because the observer did not view the scale perpendicularly.
options.D: It has no effect because the balance gives consistent readings.

answer_text: It introduces a systematic error because every measurement is shifted by the same amount.

explanation.question_restate: A beam balance has a calibration fault that makes every mass reading 0.25 g larger than the true value. The task is to identify the type of error produced by this fault.
explanation.principle:
A systematic error is a consistent error that affects measurements in the same direction and often by the same amount or proportion. It usually results from faulty calibration, instrument bias, poor experimental design or constant environmental influences. Because the error is consistent, repeated measurements remain close to one another but are all displaced from the true value.

A beginner may ask: 'If the readings are consistent, why are they still wrong?' Consistency only means the instrument gives similar results each time. If the instrument itself is incorrectly calibrated, all those similar readings can still be inaccurate.

Another beginner may ask: 'How is this different from a random error?' Random errors change unpredictably from one observation to another due to factors such as human reaction time or fluctuating environmental conditions. Systematic errors, however, have a definite pattern and can often be corrected by recalibrating the instrument.
steps:
Step 1: Identify the nature of the fault.

The beam balance always adds 0.25 g to every measurement.

Step 2: Determine whether the error changes from one reading to another.

No. The error remains constant for every object measured.

Step 3: Recall the definition of a systematic error.

A systematic error consistently shifts measurements in the same direction due to causes such as faulty calibration or instrument bias.

Step 4: Compare the situation with the definition.

Since every recorded mass is increased by the same amount, the instrument has a calibration bias.

Step 5: Conclude the correct answer.

The fault produces a systematic error.
why_other_options_are_wrong: A. Correct. A constant calibration bias causes every reading to be displaced by the same amount, which is the hallmark of a systematic error.
B. Incorrect. Random errors do not produce the same error in every measurement; they vary unpredictably.
C. Incorrect. Parallax error occurs when a scale is viewed from the wrong angle, not because of faulty instrument calibration.
D. Incorrect. Consistent readings do not guarantee correctness. An instrument can be precise yet inaccurate if it has a calibration error.
explanation.exam_tip: In JAMB Physics, keywords such as 'faulty calibration', 'instrument bias', 'zero shift', 'every reading is higher', 'every reading is lower', or 'constant error' usually indicate a systematic error. Remember that repeated measurements cannot eliminate a systematic error; the instrument must first be recalibrated or corrected.
explanation.final_answer: A. It introduces a systematic error because every measurement is shifted by the same amount.
explanation.simple_explanation: The balance always adds 0.25 g, regardless of the object being weighed. Since every reading is affected in exactly the same way, the error is systematic rather than random.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Errors, Accuracy and Significant Figures → Systematic Errors to learn how faulty calibration, instrument bias, environmental effects and poor experimental design influence measurements. Study additional JAMB-style laboratory questions involving beam balances, metre rules, thermometers, vernier calipers and micrometer screw gauges.

question: A laboratory assistant used a beam balance that had not been properly calibrated to determine the masses of several metal samples. After comparison with a standard laboratory balance, it was discovered that the beam balance consistently recorded every mass as 5 g greater than the true value. Which statement best describes this source of error?

options.A: It is a random error because the error varies unpredictably from one measurement to another.
options.B: It is a systematic error because the instrument introduces the same bias into every measurement.
options.C: It is a parallax error because the scale was viewed from an incorrect position.
options.D: It is a gross error because the observer forgot to read the balance correctly.

answer_text: It is a systematic error because the instrument introduces the same bias into every measurement.

explanation.question_restate: A beam balance consistently overestimates every measured mass by 5 g because it is poorly calibrated. The task is to identify the type of measurement error produced by this situation.
explanation.principle:
A systematic error is a consistent error that affects measurements in the same way each time. It usually arises from faulty calibration, worn instruments, incorrect experimental procedures or persistent environmental influences. Because the error has a fixed pattern, all readings are shifted either above or below the true value by approximately the same amount.

A beginner may ask: 'Why is this not a random error?' Random errors change unpredictably from one observation to another because of uncontrollable variations such as reaction time or slight environmental fluctuations. In contrast, the beam balance in this question adds the same 5 g to every measurement, showing a constant bias.

Proper calibration against a standard instrument is one of the most effective ways of detecting and correcting systematic errors in laboratory work.
steps:
Step 1: Identify the behaviour of the instrument.

Every measurement is 5 g higher than the true value.

Step 2: Determine whether the error changes from one reading to another.

No. The error remains the same for all measurements.

Step 3: Recall the definition of a systematic error.

A systematic error consistently shifts measurements in one direction due to instrument bias, faulty calibration or an experimental defect.

Step 4: Compare the observation with the definition.

Since the beam balance always adds 5 g, it introduces a constant positive bias.

Step 5: Conclude the type of error.

The error is a systematic error caused by poor calibration of the beam balance.
why_other_options_are_wrong: A. It is a random error because the error varies unpredictably from one measurement to another. — Incorrect. The error is constant rather than unpredictable.
B. It is a systematic error because the instrument introduces the same bias into every measurement. — Correct. A fixed calibration bias is the hallmark of a systematic error.

C. It is a parallax error because the scale was viewed from an incorrect position. — Incorrect. Parallax error results from improper eye position while reading a scale, not from a miscalibrated instrument.

D. It is a gross error because the observer forgot to read the balance correctly. — Incorrect. Gross errors are mistakes made by the experimenter, whereas this error originates from the instrument itself.
explanation.exam_tip: In JAMB Physics, whenever an instrument consistently reads too high or too low because of faulty calibration or a defective scale, think of a systematic error. Keywords such as 'always', 'consistently', 'every reading', or 'constant bias' usually indicate systematic error rather than random error.
explanation.final_answer: B. It is a systematic error because the instrument introduces the same bias into every measurement.
explanation.simple_explanation: The beam balance adds the same 5 g to every mass measured. Since the error is the same each time, it is not caused by chance. It is a systematic error resulting from poor calibration of the instrument.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Errors, Accuracy and Significant Figures → Systematic Errors to learn how faulty calibration, instrument bias, environmental conditions and experimental design affect measurements. Practise additional JAMB-style questions on calibration reports, beam balances, metre rules, thermometers, vernier calipers and micrometer screw gauges.

question: A laboratory group used a steel metre rule to measure the length of several metal rods immediately after the rule had been left in direct sunlight for a long time. Unknown to the group, the heated rule had expanded slightly, causing each centimetre division to become slightly longer than its standard length. Which statement best describes the error introduced into all the measurements?

options.A: It is a systematic error because the expanded scale causes all measured lengths to be consistently smaller than their true values.
options.B: It is a random error because different observers may read different values.
options.C: It is a gross error because the readings are due to careless observation.
options.D: It is a parallax error because the eye is not directly above the scale.

answer_text: It is a systematic error because the expanded scale causes all measured lengths to be consistently smaller than their true values.

explanation.question_restate: A steel metre rule becomes hotter and expands before it is used for measurement. The expansion makes each scale division slightly longer than normal. The task is to identify the type of error produced and its effect on the measured lengths.
explanation.principle:
A systematic error is a consistent error that affects measurements in the same direction each time an observation is made. It usually arises from faulty calibration, instrument bias or environmental conditions affecting the measuring instrument.

When a steel metre rule is heated, thermal expansion increases the spacing between its graduations. Consequently, one marked centimetre on the rule is actually slightly longer than one true centimetre.

Suppose a rod has a true length of 50.0 cm. Because each division on the expanded rule is longer than it should be, fewer divisions are needed to span the rod. The reading obtained will therefore be slightly less than the true length. Since the same expanded rule is used throughout the experiment, every measurement is biased in the same direction, making the error systematic rather than random.
steps:
Step 1: Identify the environmental condition affecting the instrument.

The metre rule was heated by sunlight.

Step 2: Recall the effect of heating on a metal rule.

Heating causes the steel rule to expand, increasing the distance between successive graduations.

Step 3: Determine how this affects measurements.

Because each scale division is longer than the standard length, fewer divisions are counted when measuring an object.

Step 4: Compare the measured value with the true value.

The measured length becomes consistently smaller than the actual length.

Step 5: Identify the type of error.

Since every reading is shifted in the same direction by the same underlying cause, the error is a systematic error produced by an environmental effect.
why_other_options_are_wrong: A. Correct. Thermal expansion changes the calibration of the metre rule, introducing a consistent bias that makes measured lengths smaller than their true values.
B. Random errors produce unpredictable variations from one reading to another. The expanded rule affects every measurement in the same direction, so the error is not random.
C. Gross errors arise from mistakes such as recording the wrong value or misreading the instrument. The situation described is caused by the condition of the instrument, not carelessness.

D. Parallax error occurs when the observer's eye is not correctly positioned relative to the scale. The question describes an altered scale due to thermal expansion, not an incorrect viewing position.
explanation.exam_tip: JAMB often tests systematic errors through practical situations rather than direct definitions. Watch for clues such as faulty calibration, worn scales, zero error, thermal expansion of instruments, or environmental conditions that make every reading consistently too high or too low. A consistent bias always points to a systematic error.
explanation.final_answer: A. It is a systematic error because the expanded scale causes all measured lengths to be consistently smaller than their true values.
explanation.simple_explanation: The hot metre rule expands, so each marked centimetre is slightly longer than a true centimetre. As a result, an object appears to occupy fewer centimetre divisions than it actually should. Every measurement is therefore slightly smaller than the true value. Since this happens to every reading in the same way, it is a systematic error.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Errors, Accuracy and Significant Figures → Systematic Errors to learn how calibration errors, instrument bias, thermal expansion, environmental conditions and experimental design influence measurements. Practise additional JAMB-style questions involving metre rules, vernier calipers, micrometer screw gauges and laboratory error analysis.

question: A school laboratory investigated the extension of a spring using different loads. Unknown to the laboratory assistant, the metre rule used throughout the experiment had been manufactured with scale divisions that were uniformly 1% shorter than the correct standard. All readings were carefully taken at eye level, and repeated measurements gave almost identical values. Which statement best describes the effect of this fault on the experiment?

options.A: The measurements contain a systematic error because every recorded length is consistently biased in the same direction.
options.B: The measurements contain a random error because repeated readings always vary unpredictably.
options.C: The error is caused mainly by parallax since the observer used the metre rule.
options.D: The repeated measurements eliminate the error completely because their average is taken.

answer_text: The measurements contain a systematic error because every recorded length is consistently biased in the same direction.

explanation.question_restate: A spring-extension experiment was carried out with a faulty metre rule whose scale divisions were all uniformly 1% shorter than the correct standard. The observations were repeated carefully and viewed correctly to avoid parallax. The task is to identify the type and effect of the error introduced by the faulty instrument.
explanation.principle:
A systematic error is an error that shifts every measurement in the same direction by a consistent amount or proportion. It commonly arises from faulty calibration, instrument defects, poor experimental design or persistent environmental influences. Unlike random errors, systematic errors do not fluctuate from one reading to another. Therefore, repeating the measurement or averaging several readings does not remove the bias because every reading is affected in the same way.

In this experiment, the metre rule itself is defective because every scale division is uniformly shorter than it should be. Consequently, every measured length is affected by the same calibration fault, making the error systematic rather than random.
steps:
Step 1: Identify the source of the error.

The metre rule is incorrectly manufactured, so the measuring instrument is faulty.

Step 2: Determine whether the fault affects only some readings or every reading.

Since every scale division is uniformly incorrect, every measurement is affected in the same way.

Step 3: Classify the error.

A consistent bias introduced by a faulty instrument is a systematic error.

Step 4: Consider the effect of repeating the measurements.

Repeated observations will still use the same faulty metre rule, so all readings remain biased.

Step 5: Eliminate incorrect alternatives.

The problem is not due to random fluctuations or parallax because the observer read the scale correctly at eye level.

Step 6: Conclude.

The experiment suffers from a systematic error caused by the faulty calibration of the measuring instrument.
why_other_options_are_wrong: A. The measurements contain a systematic error because every recorded length is consistently biased in the same direction. — Correct. A uniformly faulty scale introduces the same bias into every measurement.

B. The measurements contain a random error because repeated readings always vary unpredictably. — Incorrect. Random errors arise from unpredictable variations, whereas the faulty scale produces a constant bias.

C. The error is caused mainly by parallax since the observer used the metre rule. — Incorrect. The question states that readings were taken carefully at eye level, eliminating parallax as the main source of error.

D. The repeated measurements eliminate the error completely because their average is taken. — Incorrect. Averaging reduces random errors but does not remove systematic errors caused by a faulty instrument.
explanation.exam_tip: In JAMB practical questions, whenever an instrument is incorrectly calibrated, has a worn scale, or possesses a constant zero or scale defect, think of systematic error. Repeating measurements or calculating an average improves reliability against random errors but cannot correct a calibration bias. The only remedy is to recalibrate, repair or replace the faulty instrument.
explanation.final_answer: A. The measurements contain a systematic error because every recorded length is consistently biased in the same direction.
explanation.simple_explanation: The metre rule is faulty, so every length measured with it is affected by the same error. Since the error is always in the same direction, it is a systematic error. Taking many readings cannot remove this type of error because the same faulty instrument is used each time.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Errors, Accuracy and Significant Figures → Systematic Errors to learn how calibration faults, instrument bias, defective scales, environmental conditions and experimental design affect measurements. Practise additional JAMB-style laboratory questions involving metre rules, vernier calipers, micrometer screw gauges, beam balances and other measuring instruments.

question: A laboratory assistant used a stopwatch to measure the time taken for a pendulum to complete 20 oscillations. Five repeated measurements were recorded as 32.4 s, 32.7 s, 32.5 s, 32.6 s and 32.3 s. The small differences among the readings were attributed mainly to the observer's reaction time. Which procedure would best reduce the effect of this type of error when reporting the final result?

options.A: Discard all the readings except the largest value because it is likely to be the most accurate.
options.B: Use the first reading only since it was taken before fatigue could affect the observer.
options.C: Calculate the mean of all the repeated measurements before reporting the result.
options.D: Replace the stopwatch with a metre rule and repeat the experiment.

answer_text: Calculate the mean of all the repeated measurements before reporting the result.

explanation.question_restate: Several stopwatch readings of the same pendulum experiment differ slightly because of the observer's reaction time. The task is to identify the best method for reducing the influence of this random error before reporting the experimental result.
explanation.principle:
Random errors are unpredictable variations that occur from one measurement to another. They may arise from factors such as human reaction time, slight changes in observation, environmental fluctuations or instrument reading uncertainty. Unlike systematic errors, random errors do not always occur in the same direction. Some measurements may be slightly higher than the true value, while others may be slightly lower.

Because these errors vary randomly, repeating the measurement several times and calculating the arithmetic mean reduces their overall effect. The positive and negative deviations tend to cancel one another, producing a result that is closer to the true value.

In practical Physics, repeated observations and averaging are standard methods for improving the reliability of experimental results when random errors are present.
steps: Step 1: Observe that the experiment was repeated several times under the same conditions.
Step 2: Notice that the readings differ only slightly (32.3 s to 32.7 s), indicating normal random variation rather than a consistent bias.
Step 3: Recognize that the stated cause of the variation is the observer's reaction time, which is a common source of random error in stopwatch measurements.
Step 4: Recall that random errors are reduced by taking repeated measurements and calculating their mean.
Step 5: Therefore, the best procedure is to calculate the average of all the valid readings before reporting the final result.
why_other_options_are_wrong: A. Discard all the readings except the largest value because it is likely to be the most accurate. — Incorrect. There is no scientific reason to assume the largest reading is the closest to the true value.
B. Use the first reading only since it was taken before fatigue could affect the observer. — Incorrect. A single reading is more affected by random error than the average of several readings.
C. Calculate the mean of all the repeated measurements before reporting the result. — Correct. Averaging repeated observations reduces the effect of random errors caused by reaction time.
D. Replace the stopwatch with a metre rule and repeat the experiment. — Incorrect. A metre rule measures length, not time, so it cannot be used for this experiment.
explanation.exam_tip: Whenever JAMB mentions repeated measurements with small unpredictable differences caused by reaction time, reading uncertainty or minor environmental changes, think of random error. The standard remedy is to repeat the measurements and calculate their mean. Do not confuse this with systematic error, which cannot be removed simply by averaging repeated observations.
explanation.final_answer: C. Calculate the mean of all the repeated measurements before reporting the result.
explanation.simple_explanation: The stopwatch readings are slightly different because reaction time varies from one trial to another. Since these differences are random, the best way to obtain a more reliable result is to average all the readings. This reduces the effect of the random fluctuations.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Errors, Accuracy and Significant Figures → Random Errors to learn how repeated observations, averaging, reaction time, environmental fluctuations and reading uncertainties affect laboratory measurements. Practise additional JAMB-style questions involving stopwatch experiments, pendulum investigations and repeated measurements.

question: A laboratory assistant measured the time taken for a simple pendulum to complete 20 oscillations five times using the same stopwatch. The recorded times were 35.8 s, 36.1 s, 35.9 s, 36.0 s and 35.7 s. The pendulum and experimental setup remained unchanged throughout the exercise. Which statement best explains the small differences in the recorded times?

options.A: The stopwatch had a constant zero error that shifted every reading by the same amount.
options.B: The variation was caused by random errors arising from factors such as human reaction time.
options.C: The length of the pendulum changed significantly after each trial.
options.D: The stopwatch was incorrectly calibrated, making each reading progressively larger.

answer_text: The variation was caused by random errors arising from factors such as human reaction time.

explanation.question_restate: Five repeated measurements of the time for 20 oscillations of the same pendulum are slightly different. The task is to identify the most likely reason for these small variations.
explanation.principle:
Random errors are unpredictable variations that occur when repeated measurements are made under apparently identical conditions. They commonly arise from unavoidable factors such as slight differences in human reaction time, tiny environmental changes, or uncertainty in reading an instrument.

A beginner may ask: 'Why are the readings not exactly the same if nothing changed?' No measurement is perfectly repeatable. Even when the apparatus is unchanged, small uncontrollable factors cause slight differences between observations.

Another beginner may ask: 'How are random errors different from systematic errors?' Random errors cause measurements to scatter above and below the true value, whereas systematic errors shift measurements consistently in one direction because of a fault such as poor calibration or zero error.

The readings in this question vary only slightly around about 36.0 s without showing a consistent increase or decrease. This pattern is characteristic of random error rather than systematic error.
steps:
Step 1: Examine the repeated measurements.

35.8 s, 36.1 s, 35.9 s, 36.0 s and 35.7 s are all close to one another.

Step 2: Look for a pattern.

The readings fluctuate both above and below approximately 36.0 s instead of changing consistently in one direction.

Step 3: Recall the characteristics of random error.

Random errors produce small unpredictable differences between repeated measurements under the same conditions.

Step 4: Identify a likely source.

When using a stopwatch, slight differences in starting and stopping times due to human reaction are a common source of random error.

Step 5: Eliminate other possibilities.

A constant zero error or faulty calibration would introduce a systematic error, causing all readings to be shifted consistently rather than scattered.
Step 6: Conclude that the observed variation is best explained by random errors associated with repeated timing measurements.
why_other_options_are_wrong: A. The stopwatch had a constant zero error that shifted every reading by the same amount. — Incorrect. A constant zero error is a systematic error and would affect every reading equally rather than producing random scatter.

B. The variation was caused by random errors arising from factors such as human reaction time. — Correct. Small unpredictable differences between repeated stopwatch measurements are typical of random errors.

C. The length of the pendulum changed significantly after each trial. — Incorrect. If the pendulum length changed significantly, much larger and more systematic changes in the measured times would be expected.
D. The stopwatch was incorrectly calibrated, making each reading progressively larger. — Incorrect. The readings do not increase progressively; they fluctuate around a common value.
explanation.exam_tip: JAMB often tests random errors using repeated laboratory observations rather than asking for a direct definition. When repeated measurements fluctuate slightly above and below a central value, think of random error. A consistent shift in one direction usually indicates a systematic error such as zero error or poor calibration.
explanation.final_answer: B. The variation was caused by random errors arising from factors such as human reaction time.
explanation.simple_explanation: The recorded times are all close to 36.0 s but are not exactly the same. Such small, unpredictable differences commonly occur because a person cannot start and stop a stopwatch at exactly the same instant every time. This is a random error, so option B is correct.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Errors, Accuracy and Significant Figures → Random Errors to learn how repeated observations are analysed, how random errors differ from systematic errors, why averaging improves reliability, and how these ideas are applied in JAMB practical questions involving stopwatches, pendulums and other laboratory measurements.

question: A laboratory assistant used a stopwatch to determine the time taken for a simple pendulum to complete 20 oscillations. Five trials gave the following times: 35.8 s, 36.2 s, 35.9 s, 36.1 s and 36.0 s. Assuming the stopwatch is correctly calibrated, which procedure gives the best estimate of the true time and reduces the effect of random errors?

options.A: Record the smallest measured time, 35.8 s, because it is least affected by delay.
options.B: Record the largest measured time, 36.2 s, because it is closest to the actual value.
options.C: Calculate the mean of the five readings and use it as the final result.
options.D: Discard all readings except the last one because the observer becomes more experienced.

answer_text: Calculate the mean of the five readings and use it as the final result.

explanation.question_restate: Several stopwatch measurements of the same event differ slightly because of random errors. The task is to identify the best way to obtain the most reliable estimate of the true time.
explanation.principle:
Random errors are unpredictable variations that occur from one measurement to another. They commonly arise from factors such as human reaction time, slight changes in observation, or minor environmental fluctuations. Unlike systematic errors, random errors do not consistently increase or decrease the measured value.

A beginner may ask: 'Why are the readings different if the experiment is the same?' Small unavoidable variations occur each time a measurement is taken, especially when using a handheld stopwatch.

Another beginner may ask: 'How can these random errors be reduced?' The accepted scientific method is to repeat the measurement several times under the same conditions and calculate the arithmetic mean. Positive and negative deviations tend to cancel one another, giving a value that is closer to the true value.

This principle is widely applied in laboratory experiments and is frequently tested in JAMB through practical measurement scenarios.
steps: Step 1: Observe that five measurements of the same quantity have been recorded.
Step 2: Notice that the readings vary only slightly (35.8 s to 36.2 s), which is typical of random errors.
Step 3: Since the stopwatch is stated to be correctly calibrated, there is no evidence of a systematic error.
Step 4: The best estimate of the true value is obtained by averaging repeated measurements.

Step 5: Calculate the mean if required:

Mean = (35.8 + 36.2 + 35.9 + 36.1 + 36.0) ÷ 5 = 180.0 ÷ 5 = 36.0 s.
Step 6: Therefore, the appropriate procedure is to report the mean value of the repeated observations.
Step 7: This method reduces the influence of random errors and improves the reliability of the reported result.
why_other_options_are_wrong: A. Record the smallest measured time — Incorrect. Choosing the lowest reading ignores the other valid observations and does not reduce random errors.
B. Record the largest measured time — Incorrect. There is no scientific basis for assuming the highest reading is closest to the true value.

C. Calculate the mean of the five readings and use it as the final result. — Correct. Averaging repeated observations reduces the effect of random errors and provides the best estimate of the true value.
D. Discard all readings except the last one — Incorrect. A single reading is generally less reliable than the average of several repeated measurements.
explanation.exam_tip: Whenever repeated measurements differ only slightly and no faulty instrument is indicated, think of random errors. In JAMB practical questions, the correct response is usually to repeat the measurements and calculate the mean rather than selecting the highest, lowest or last reading. Remember that averaging reduces random errors but does not eliminate systematic errors.
explanation.final_answer: C. Calculate the mean of the five readings and use it as the final result.
explanation.simple_explanation: The stopwatch readings are not exactly the same because of random errors such as slight differences in reaction time. The best way to reduce these random variations is to repeat the measurement and calculate the average. The mean of the five readings is 36.0 s, making option C the correct choice.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Errors, Accuracy and Significant Figures → Random Errors to learn how repeated measurements, averaging, uncertainty analysis and reliability are applied in practical physics. Explore additional JAMB-style laboratory questions involving stopwatches, pendulums, thermometers, voltmeters and other measuring instruments.

question: A laboratory assistant measured the time taken for a simple pendulum to complete 20 oscillations five times under identical conditions. The recorded times were 36.4 s, 36.8 s, 36.5 s, 36.7 s and 36.6 s. The slight differences between the readings were attributed to unavoidable variations in starting and stopping the stopwatch. Which procedure would best reduce the effect of these random errors when reporting the final result?

options.A: Use the largest recorded time because it is the safest estimate.
options.B: Calculate the mean of all the recorded times.
options.C: Discard all readings except the first measurement.
options.D: Subtract 0.2 s from every recorded value before reporting.

answer_text: Calculate the mean of all the recorded times.

explanation.question_restate: Five measurements of the time for 20 oscillations of a pendulum differ slightly because of unavoidable stopwatch timing variations. The task is to identify the best method for reducing the influence of random errors on the reported result.
explanation.principle: Random errors are unpredictable variations that occur from one measurement to another even when the same procedure is repeated. They commonly arise from human reaction time, slight environmental changes or limitations in reading instruments. Since these errors fluctuate above and below the true value, taking several measurements and calculating their mean reduces their overall influence. Averaging does not eliminate random errors completely, but it produces a value that is more reliable than any single measurement. This is why repeated observations and averaging are standard practice in experimental physics.
steps: Step 1: Observe that the recorded times are close to one another but not identical: 36.4 s, 36.8 s, 36.5 s, 36.7 s and 36.6 s.
Step 2: Recognize that the differences are caused by unpredictable stopwatch timing variations, which are examples of random errors.
Step 3: Recall that random errors cannot be corrected by adding or subtracting a fixed value because they do not have a consistent pattern.
Step 4: The accepted method of reducing random errors is to repeat the measurement several times.

Step 5: Calculate the arithmetic mean of all valid readings.

Mean = (36.4 + 36.8 + 36.5 + 36.7 + 36.6) ÷ 5 = 183.0 ÷ 5 = 36.6 s.
Step 6: Report the mean value, since it provides the most reliable estimate of the measured quantity.
Step 7: Therefore, the correct procedure is to calculate the mean of all the recorded times.
why_other_options_are_wrong: A. Use the largest recorded time because it is the safest estimate. — Incorrect. Choosing the largest value introduces bias and ignores the purpose of repeated measurements.
B. Calculate the mean of all the recorded times. — Correct. Averaging repeated observations reduces the effect of random errors and gives the most reliable estimate.
C. Discard all readings except the first measurement. — Incorrect. Using only one reading increases the influence of random error instead of reducing it.
D. Subtract 0.2 s from every recorded value before reporting. — Incorrect. A fixed correction is used for systematic errors, not random errors, which vary unpredictably.
explanation.exam_tip: When JAMB describes repeated measurements that differ slightly because of reaction time, fluctuating conditions or reading uncertainty, the expected answer is usually to calculate the mean of the valid readings. Do not confuse random errors with systematic errors. Systematic errors require calibration or correction, whereas random errors are minimized through repeated measurements and averaging.
explanation.final_answer: B. Calculate the mean of all the recorded times.
explanation.simple_explanation: Each stopwatch reading is slightly different because of random error. Since these variations are unpredictable, the best estimate is obtained by taking the average of all the readings. The mean time is 36.6 s, making averaging the correct procedure.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Errors, Accuracy and Significant Figures → Random Errors to learn how repeated observations, statistical averaging and careful experimental techniques improve the reliability of laboratory measurements. Study additional JAMB-style questions involving stopwatches, pendulums, electrical measurements and repeated experimental observations.

question: While determining the period of oscillation of a simple pendulum, a laboratory assistant measured the time taken for 20 oscillations five times and obtained 40.2 s, 39.8 s, 40.1 s, 39.9 s and 40.0 s. The small differences among these readings are attributed mainly to random errors. Which procedure gives the most reliable estimate of the time for 20 oscillations?

options.A: Record the largest reading because it is likely to be the closest to the true value.
options.B: Use the first reading only since it was taken before fatigue could affect the observer.
options.C: Calculate the mean of the five readings.
options.D: Discard all the readings and repeat the experiment with a different stopwatch.

answer_text: Calculate the mean of the five readings.

explanation.question_restate: Five measurements of the time for 20 oscillations differ slightly because of random errors. The task is to identify the best method of obtaining the most reliable value from these repeated measurements.
explanation.principle:
Random errors are unpredictable variations that occur from one measurement to another due to factors such as human reaction time, slight environmental changes, or limitations in reading an instrument. Unlike systematic errors, they do not consistently increase or decrease the measured value.

A beginner may ask: 'Why are the readings different even though the same experiment was performed?' Small unavoidable variations occur each time a measurement is made. For example, a person may start or stop a stopwatch a fraction of a second earlier or later.

Another beginner may ask: 'How can these random errors be reduced?' The accepted scientific practice is to repeat the measurement several times and calculate the arithmetic mean. Positive and negative deviations tend to cancel one another, making the average a better estimate of the true value.

The mean time is calculated as:

Mean = (40.2 + 39.8 + 40.1 + 39.9 + 40.0) ÷ 5 = 200.0 ÷ 5 = 40.0 s.

Therefore, averaging repeated observations improves the reliability of experimental results and is a standard laboratory practice frequently tested by JAMB.
steps: Step 1: Recognise that the readings differ only slightly, indicating random rather than systematic errors.

Step 2: Add all five measurements.

40.2 + 39.8 + 40.1 + 39.9 + 40.0 = 200.0 s.

Step 3: Divide the total by the number of observations.

Mean = 200.0 ÷ 5 = 40.0 s.
Step 4: Understand that averaging reduces the effect of random fluctuations because unusually high and low readings tend to balance one another.
Step 5: Conclude that the mean value provides the most reliable estimate of the time for 20 oscillations.
why_other_options_are_wrong: A. Record the largest reading because it is likely to be the closest to the true value. — Incorrect. There is no scientific basis for assuming the largest reading is the most accurate.
B. Use the first reading only since it was taken before fatigue could affect the observer. — Incorrect. A single reading is more susceptible to random error than the average of repeated measurements.
C. Calculate the mean of the five readings. — Correct. Averaging repeated observations reduces the influence of random errors and gives the most reliable estimate.
D. Discard all the readings and repeat the experiment with a different stopwatch. — Incorrect. The readings are consistent and suitable for averaging; changing the stopwatch is unnecessary.
explanation.exam_tip:
Whenever repeated measurements show small scatter without any obvious outlier, the best estimate is the arithmetic mean. JAMB commonly tests this idea in experiments involving stopwatches, pendulums, metre rules, thermometers and electrical instruments.

Do not confuse random errors with systematic errors. Random errors are reduced by repeating measurements and averaging, whereas systematic errors require identifying and correcting the source of the bias.
explanation.final_answer: C. Calculate the mean of the five readings.
explanation.simple_explanation: The readings are all close to one another, showing only small random variations. The accepted way to reduce the effect of these random errors is to calculate the average of all the measurements. The mean of the five readings is 40.0 s, making averaging the most reliable procedure.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Errors, Accuracy and Significant Figures → Random Errors to learn how repeated measurements improve reliability, how to identify random and systematic errors, and how laboratory data are analysed in JAMB-style practical questions. question: A laboratory assistant tested the consistency of a digital balance using a standard mass of 100.00 g. The balance produced the following readings after five measurements: 99.98 g, 100.01 g, 100.00 g, 99.99 g and 100.02 g. Which conclusion best describes the performance of the balance?

options.A: The balance is accurate but not precise because the readings are close to the true value but differ from one another.
options.B: The balance is precise but not accurate because the readings are close together but far from the accepted value.
options.C: The balance is both accurate and precise because the readings are close to one another and close to the accepted value.
options.D: The balance is neither accurate nor precise because the readings are not exactly 100.00 g.

answer_text: The balance is both accurate and precise because the readings are close to one another and close to the accepted value.

explanation.question_restate: A digital balance was used repeatedly to measure a standard mass of 100.00 g. The readings obtained were 99.98 g, 100.01 g, 100.00 g, 99.99 g and 100.02 g. The task is to determine whether the balance demonstrates accuracy, precision, both, or neither.
explanation.principle:
Accuracy describes how close a measured value is to the accepted or true value, while precision describes how close repeated measurements are to one another. A measuring instrument may be accurate without being precise, precise without being accurate, both accurate and precise, or neither.

A beginner may ask: 'What is the accepted value?' It is the known correct value against which measurements are compared. In this question, the accepted mass is 100.00 g.

Another beginner may ask: 'How do I know if the readings are precise?' Examine whether the repeated readings are tightly grouped. Here, all five readings lie within 0.04 g of each other, showing excellent consistency.

To determine accuracy, compare the readings with the accepted value. Since they are all extremely close to 100.00 g, the balance is also accurate.

JAMB frequently tests the distinction between accuracy and precision using practical laboratory situations rather than asking candidates to memorise definitions.
steps:
Step 1: Identify the accepted (true) value.

Accepted mass = 100.00 g.

Step 2: Examine the spread of the repeated readings.

The readings vary only from 99.98 g to 100.02 g, a total spread of 0.04 g. This indicates high precision because the measurements are very consistent.

Step 3: Compare the readings with the accepted value.

Each reading is extremely close to 100.00 g, indicating high accuracy.

Step 4: Combine both observations.

Since the readings are both tightly grouped and close to the accepted value, the balance is both accurate and precise.

Step 5: Select the correct conclusion.

The correct option is C.
why_other_options_are_wrong: A. The balance is accurate but not precise — Incorrect. The readings are not widely scattered; they are closely grouped, so the balance is precise as well.
B. The balance is precise but not accurate — Incorrect. Although the readings are consistent, they are also very close to the accepted value, so the balance is accurate.
C. The balance is both accurate and precise — Correct. The measurements are clustered closely together and centred on the accepted mass of 100.00 g.

D. The balance is neither accurate nor precise — Incorrect. In real laboratory work, repeated measurements are rarely exactly identical. Small variations are expected due to unavoidable measurement uncertainty.
explanation.exam_tip:
A common JAMB trap is to confuse accuracy with precision.

Remember:

• Accuracy = closeness to the true or accepted value.
• Precision = closeness of repeated measurements to one another.

If repeated readings are tightly grouped around the accepted value, the instrument is both accurate and precise.

Do not assume that an instrument is inaccurate simply because repeated readings are not exactly identical.
explanation.final_answer: C. The balance is both accurate and precise because the readings are close to one another and close to the accepted value.
explanation.simple_explanation: The five readings are almost the same, so the balance is precise. They are also all very close to the correct mass of 100.00 g, so the balance is accurate. Therefore, the balance is both accurate and precise.
